Protest and State Response in China

POLI3148 Data Science in Politics and Public Administration

Analytical Report

Zhu Qile

1. Introduction

China has developed one of the most sophisticated surveillance and security infrastructures in the world. Yet data shows that demonstrations and even riots continue to occur across the country every year. What are protests like in such an environment? How does the state respond to them? These questions lead to the current research. Numerous scholars have suggested that the answer lies not in uniform repression but in selective responses: the state intervenes forcefully in some cases while remaining others untouched; moreover, even among the intervened cases, the form of intervention varies from policing to military crackdown (Lorentzen, 2007; Mattingly, 2019). However, there lacks a systematic review of the state’s rationale of intervention, and this report serves as a summarizing attempt.

The report provides a data-backed overview of protests in mainland China between 2018 and 2025 and analyzes how the state's response varies across different dimensions. Drawing on 8,937 events from the Armed Conflict Location and Event Data (ACLED) project, it reveals a pattern of selective coercion that illuminates the logic of authoritarian governance in contemporary China.

2. Research Question

This report is organized around two core research questions:

  1. How are protests distributed in China?
  2. How does the Chinese state adjust its response to different protests?

These questions treat protest as both a phenomenon to be mapped and a political challenge to be managed. The first examines the structural patterns of collective action — where protests occur, who participates, what drives them, and when they happen. The second treats state response as an outcome shaped by the perceived threat to regime stability and examines how four factors affect that response: the level of collective violence, crowd size, the identity of protesters, and geographic location.

3. Data and Method

This report uses the Armed Conflict Location and Event Data (ACLED) as the dataset. As an authoritative dataset of conflicts around the world, ACLED systematically codes political violence and protest events using various data sources (ACLED, n.d.). After filtering for protests in mainland China, the dataset yields 8,937 events from January 2018 to April 2025. The dataset records each event's date, location, type, actors, crowd size, and a short textual description. Unless otherwise specified, "protest" in this report refers to both “protest” and “riot” (which involves collective violence) in ACLED’s typology of events

Crowd size data were recoded from inconsistent textual formats into standardized numeric values following the rules detailed in the Appendix. Text data in the event description part was processed through tokenization, lemmatization, and customized stop words removal. Multiple analytical methods are employed, including descriptive summaries, logistic regression, and automated text analysis. All analyses and visualizations were conducted in Python.

4. Findings

Protests in China

4.1 Overview

As Figure 1 and 2 show, most protests in China involve no violence from the protesters’ side, and violent riots account for only 2.5% of the total events. As for crowd size, majority of protests have a medium size between 51 and 200 people, with rare large-scale ones that have over 1000 participants. These show that the modal protest in China is a peaceful, contained, and localized demonstration.

Geographically, protests cluster heavily in China's eastern and southern coastal cities, with Shenzhen, Xi'an, Beijing, Shanghai, and Guangzhou recording the highest counts. These national or regional economic hubs have likely attracted a large population seeking career opportunities and better quality of life. Consequently, collective mobilization becomes much easier compared with cities with lower population densities. It is also worth noting that the most prevalent protest actors identified below — labor workers, teachers, and students — are typically concentrated in these economically prosperous cities, which further explains why protest activity is so geographically skewed toward the coast.

4.2 Who Protests, and Why?

Labor protests hold a supermajority among all events. There’s three mutually reinforcing explanations for this. First, China's relatively weak labor protection leaves workers with few institutional channels to resolve disputes. Second, unpaid wages pose a severe subsistence threat for migrant construction workers, whose family incomes largely depend on their remittances, giving them an unusually strong incentive to act collectively. Third, as the state response section below shows, labor protests face the lowest intervention rates, making demonstration a comparatively low-risk strategy.

The word cloud further details protesters’ motives. The dominant "wage," "arrear," and "owe" reveal the major incentive: economic disputes. Not only manual workers face back pays, but teachers, health workers (especially the temporary helpers during the pandemic), and retired soldiers also demonstrate for salary delays, job cuts, and retirement benefits. This picture of protests is very different from those driven by political advocacies.

4.3 When Do Protests Happen?

Figure 7 shows that protest frequency dropped sharply in early 2020 as COVID-19 lockdowns restricted physical assembly, then gradually recovered. Moreover, the actor-based summary in Figure 8 reveal a distinct trend covered up by the overall changes. The student spike in late 2022 corresponds to the widespread anti-lockdown protests (also called the White Paper Movement) — a rare political protest oriented directly against the party-state system. Health worker protests also surged in the aftermath of the pandemic, possibly due to the layoffs and arrears resulting from the excessive expenditure on “Zero-Covid”. Both underscore the heightened social contention that characterized the last days of the “Zero-COVID” era (An et al., 2022).

The monthly count manifests a distinct seasonality of pretests, with demonstrations peak from November to January. The year-end peak is likely driven by the customary settlement of arrears ahead of the Chinese New Year, a high-pressure period where the back pays frequently catalyze collective action (Xinhua News Agency, 2024). This often-overlooked pattern highlights how cultural tradition may shape demonstration decisions.

State Response

4.4 Overview

In this study, state intervention is defined as the presence of a named counter party. The overall intervention rate is 9.7%, and the dominant intervening force is the civilian police. The rarity of the armed police and the militia suggests that the state's first-line instrument for protest management is regular policing rather than militarized force, which minimizes the public backlash that might arise from brutal repressions.

4.5 Violence Level and State Intervention

Figure 11 reveals the most dramatic variation: while most protests go uncontested, riots trigger intervention in over 90% of cases, with more diverse intervention forces employed. This is possibly because that the presence of violence could cause severer damage to socio-economic order. Riots also carry a contagion risk — they signal that citizens could defy state authority with violence, a precedent that must be rapidly extinguished.

4.6 Crowd Size

To isolate crowd size effects, the logistic regression in Figure 12 is restricted to labor group protests, excludes riots, and removes extreme values (crowd size below 10 or above 10,000). A positive relationship between crowd size and intervention probability emerges, and a potential mechanism is that larger crowds pose more physical threat to both the regime and the socio-economic order. In. addition, mass gatherings also facilitate networking among participants. This heightens the likability of a spillover effect, meaning that localized events can potentially transform into broader social movements (Meyer & Whittier, 1994).

4.7 Protest Actor

Figure 13 reveals a striking pattern: intervention rate ranges dramatically across different protest actors. At the top are former military personnel and ethnic minorities. Veterans present a dual threat: physically, they’re equipped with military experience; ideologically, their status as former state servants grants them moral legitimacy and in turn harms the state’s reputation. Moreover, their presence risks undermining the loyalty of current soldiers, which threatens the state’s military power. For ethnic groups, their activism is perceived as an explicit challenge to the state’s absolute sovereignty, which is of top sensitivity in China’s political context. Ethnic grievances also carry a high risk of spillover, as shared identity can catalyze nationwide demonstrations and draw international attention (Meyer & Whittier, 1994).

Next are religious groups, women, and farmers. Both religious group and feminist protesters pose an ideological challenge: rather than confronting the state directly, they sustain alternative ideological frameworks that “erode” the secular and conservative official order, as the 2015 detention of the "Feminist Five" illustrated (Wang, 2015). Farmers are an outlier, as their land-dispute protests rarely threatens the state. An explanation is that village population is sparser, therefore suppression risks less central scrutiny and public backlash compared with those in cities (Göbel, 2021).

Labor groups suffer from the lowest intervention rate. Their economic disputes can be resolved without state force. Tolerating them also serves the party's ideological self-presentation: in a self-declared socialist state, suppressing workers would shake public trust. And with labor accounting for nearly 90% of all recorded events, frequent intervention would bring huge and unnecessary operational costs.

4.8 Regional Variation

Figure 14 reveals substantial geographic variation. Tibetan area, including both Tibet Autonomous Region and Qinghai Province, records the highest intervention rate, consistent with the Tibetan ethnic activism. Conversely, Xinjiang shows a low rate. An explanation is that compared with Tibet, Xinjiang’s security measures and total press control is stricter, making it much harder for protests to form and repression to be reported. Yet this puzzle deserves further empirical answers.

Intervention rate in Beijing and Shanghai are much higher than most provincial regions. As China's premier economic hubs, disruption of socio-economic order in both cities is less likely to be tolerated by the state. Moreover, as the capital, Beijing is equipped with elevated political security, as protests may threaten, or at least alarm the central leaders. Hubei is another outlier. As the province where COVID first emerged, it was once subject to some of the strictest lockdown measures, placing local authorities on high alert of population gatherings.

5. Limitations

Four limitations should be noted. First, ACLED relies on media sources, and China’s strict information control can cause omissions and inaccuracies. Second, the standardized crowd size data involve approximation, meaning there’s a certain degree of deviation from real data. Third, the dataset begins in 2018, limiting the historical perspective. Finally, the analyses are summative or correlational rather than causal: the explanations offered are theoretically motivated interpretations, not claims backed by causal inference.

6. Appendix

  1. Crowd Size Recoding Rules: Exact numbers were retained unchanged. Approximate expressions ("around," "about," "nearly") were mapped to their anchor value; bounded ranges were converted to midpoints. "Up to" and "at least" were set to the stated threshold; "more than/over" values were multiplied by 1.1. Qualitative magnitudes ("dozens," "hundreds," "thousands," "a few/several + unit") were assigned consistent numeric proxies. Non-informative entries ("no report," "unknown") were coded as missing and excluded from quantitative analyses.

7. References

An, X., Xiao, L., Yang, X., Tang, X., & Liang, X. H. (2022). Economic burden of public health care and hospitalisation associated with COVID-19 in China. Public Health, 203, 65–74.

Armed Conflict Location and Event Data (ACLED). (n.d.). ACLED codebook. https://acleddata.com/methodology/acled-codebook

Göbel, C. (2021). The Political Logic of Protest Repression in China. Journal of Contemporary China, 30(128), 169–185. https://doi.org/10.1080/10670564.2020.1790897

Lorentzen, P. L. (2007). Regularizing rioting: Permitting public protest in an authoritarian regime. Quarterly Journal of Political Science, 8(2), 127–158. http://dx.doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.995330

Mattingly, D. (2019). The art of political control in China. Cambridge University Press.

Meyer, D. S. & Whittier, N. (1994). Social movement spillover. Social Problems, 41(2), 277-298. http://dx.doi.org/10.1525/sp.1994.41.2.03x0438v

Wang, Z. (2015). Detention of the feminist five in China. Feminist Studies, 41(2), 476-482. https://www.jstor.org/stable/10.15767/feministstudies.41.2.476

Chinese courts initiate special winter action to tackle wage arrears. https://english.court.gov.cn/2024-12/26/c_1059728.htm#:~:text=26103-,Chinese%20courts%20initiate%20special%20winter%20action%20to%20tackle%20wage%20arrears,the%20recovery%20of%20unpaid%20wages.
